planting.note: Plant grafted trees or bare-root saplings in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; azarole is a Mediterranean hawthorn with unusually large, sweet, edible fruits used fresh and in preserves throughout southern Europe and the Middle East.

directions: Plant grafted trees or container-grown saplings in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; azarole is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in the dry, hot summers of the Mediterranean basin and similar climates in the American South and West. Crataegus azarolus is a large shrub or small tree native to southern Europe and western Asia cultivated for millennia for its edible fruits — unusually large for a hawthorn (up to 3/4 inch across), round to oblong, and ripening yellow, orange, or red depending on selection, with sweet to slightly tart, apple-like flesh used fresh, in jams, jellies, liqueurs, and preserves throughout Italy, Spain, and the Middle East. The profuse white spring flowers and ornamental fruits make it as attractive as it is useful; it is well adapted to alkaline, dry, rocky soils. The Arabic name for this species, "za'rour" or "azaroul," is the origin of the English name.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees or container-grown saplings in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chinese hawthorn produces the largest-fruited hawthorn berries available in cultivation and is widely grown as an orchard tree in northern China.

directions: Plant grafted trees or container saplings in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chinese hawthorn is extremely cold-hardy, surviving temperatures to -30°F, and is one of the most productive and large-fruited hawthorns available for northern gardeners. Crataegus pinnatifida is the most commercially important hawthorn in China where it is grown in extensive orchards for its unusually large (up to 1 inch across), bright-red, shiny fruits with white-speckled skin; the tart, apple-like fruits are eaten fresh, candied (as Chinese candied hawthorn skewers — tanghulu), made into jams, dried, and used extensively in traditional Chinese medicine for digestive health. The tree is heavily thorned, produces profuse white spring flowers, and has attractive, deeply lobed leaves with good fall color. It adapts readily to a wide range of soils and climates in zones 3–8.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or bare-root plants in spring in any well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; blackthorn is an extremely tough, thorny, wildlife shrub excellent as a stock-proof hedge and for its small, tart, blue-black fruits used in sloe gin and preserves.

directions: Plant container-grown shrubs or bare-root plants in spring in any well-drained soil — poor, thin, alkaline, or rocky — in full sun to partial shade; blackthorn is one of the most adaptable and tough woody plants available, tolerating exposure, drought, poor soil, and neglect. Prunus spinosa is a very thorny, dense, suckering European native shrub that flowers brilliantly in early spring with masses of small, white blossoms on bare black stems before the leaves appear, creating a spectacular display; the flowers are followed by small (1/3–1/2 inch), round, blue-black fruits (sloes) with a white bloom, astringent flesh, and complex flavor that is unpalatable raw but transforms with frost and sugar into excellent jams, jellies, wine, and the essential ingredient of sloe gin. The dense, thorny thickets provide superb nesting habitat for birds and small mammals. Harvest sloes after the first frost for best flavor.

planting.note: Plant grafted or bare-root trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; damson plum is more tolerant of thin, alkaline, and dry soils than most plums and produces astringent, aromatic, dark-purple fruit exceptional for preserves.

directions: Plant grafted or bare-root trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; damson is more self-fertile than most plums and often sets good crops without a pollinator, though cropping improves with cross-pollination from another plum. Prunus instititia is the wild source of the cultivated damson, a small tree or large shrub producing small, oval, blue-black to purple plums in late summer with dense, richly flavored, astringent flesh quite distinct in character from the sweet, fresh-eating Japanese plums; damsons are the traditional plum for cooking, being too astringent to eat in quantity raw but transforming into superb jam (damson jam is one of Britain's most prized traditional preserves), jelly, cheese, wine, and gin. The tree is extremely long-lived, hardy, and productive even in cold, exposed, and acid soils where other plums fail.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Ussurian pear is the most cold-hardy of all pear species and is widely used as a rootstock and breeding parent for ultra-cold-hardy hybrid pears in Canada and northern US.

directions: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Ussurian pear tolerates temperatures to -40°F and is the hardiest pear species available, making it the only viable pear for zones 2–3 where European pears cannot survive. Pyrus ussuriensis is a large, ornamental, extremely cold-hardy pear species from northeastern China and Siberia; it bears masses of white flowers in early spring before the leaves, followed in fall by small (1–2 inch), round, green to yellow-green, gritty-fleshed fruits with a rough texture that are best cooked or used for preserves rather than fresh eating. Its primary agricultural value is as a rootstock for grafted pear varieties and as a breeding parent for cold-hardy pear cultivars (e.g., 'Ure', 'Rescue', 'David', 'Nova') developed by Canadian and university breeders for the northern Great Plains. The glossy, dark-green leaves turn brilliant red in fall, making it an outstanding ornamental even where fruit quality is secondary.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or bare-root plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Utah serviceberry is the most drought-tolerant of the native serviceberries and is excellent for arid western landscapes and wildlife gardens.

directions: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean, rocky, or sandy soil in full sun to light shade; Utah serviceberry thrives in dry, rocky, alkaline soils of the West and Great Basin where other serviceberries would fail. Amelanchier utahensis is a native shrub of dry foothills, rocky slopes, and canyon walls from the Great Basin west, producing white spring flowers, small (1/3 inch) dark-blue berries with a sweet, mild, blueberry-like flavor in early summer (ripe very early, often by late May-June), and outstanding fall color in shades of orange and red. The berries are an extremely important wildlife food — essentially every frugivorous bird and mammal in the arid West relies on serviceberries during the brief ripening window. Extremely drought- and cold-hardy, this is the recommended serviceberry for xeric and high-altitude western gardens. Use for wildlife plantings, erosion control, and native landscaping.
